github.name: Cognitive-Core-Skills
github.description: A universal, industry-neutral taxonomy of cognitive core skills (perception, memory, reasoning, planning, action, verification, learning, governance) for LLMs, SLMs, AI agents, and world models — with schemas, 159 skill cards, benchmarks, and CI.

github.name: Awesome-Scientific-LLM-Benchmarks
github.description: A curated, accuracy-first list of benchmarks for evaluating LLMs on scientific reasoning and discovery — math, physics, chemistry, materials, biology, and agentic science.

github.name: mpesa_safaricom-fraud_anomaly_detection
github.description: Real-time fraud detection engine for M-Pesa mobile money — Kafka/Flink streaming, ML-based risk scoring, and explainable multi-signal checks (velocity, SIM swap correlation, mule networks) feeding an interaction-aware decision engine, with a full production pipeline from ingestion through dbt analytics.

github.name: fucina
github.description: CPU-first tensor/autograd runtime and LLM inference engine in pure Zig — eager execution, GGUF in and out, LoRA training on CPU, benchmarked pairwise against llama.cpp.

github.name: topological-ml-toolkit
github.description: Rust + Python Topological ML Toolkit for persistent homology, Betti-curve features, manifold embeddings, topology docs, backend contracts, and E2E benchmarks for sklearn/PyTorch/TensorFlow-style pipelines.

github.name: creatorhub
github.description: 多平台内容监控·采集·搬运 —— 纯 Python(FastAPI + Playwright),一个 Web 面板管起抖音 / 小红书 / 快手

github.name: AMRouter
github.description: Self-hosted AI gateway — one endpoint, many providers, auto-fallback. OpenAI-compatible REST API with Cloudflare Workers AI automation.

github.name: Homekit
github.description: Homekit gives any AI agent direct, physical control over Apple Home. Flip lights. Lock doors. Read sensors. Through a single open interface.

github.name: OmniPost-AI
github.description: AI-powered Chrome extension for generating and publishing posts to Facebook, Threads, and X using ChatGPT or Gemini.

github.name: a-fable-of-codexes
github.description: Claude Code skills that make Claude (Fable) the conductor of AI worker fleets: many parallel OpenAI Codex workers plus Opus design agents, organized into campaigns, waves, squads, and review gates.

trends: Today's tech landscape is profoundly shaped by the rapid advancement and practical application of **Agentic AI**. There's a strong focus on building AI agents with defined 'cognitive core skills' (perception, reasoning, planning, action) and robust evaluation methodologies to ensure their reliability and performance, especially in scientific and complex problem-solving domains. Performance optimization for LLMs, including CPU-first inference and addressing quadratic costs, remains critical. In automation and productivity, AI agents are enabling **multi-platform content orchestration**, intelligent data access (bypassing traditional database bottlenecks), and **physical world control** through interfaces like Homekit. A parallel trend highlights a growing developer sentiment towards **self-sovereignty in tooling** and infrastructure, alongside a deep emphasis on continuous learning, reading foundational documentation, and personal discipline for sustained career growth.

ideas.title: AI Agent "Skill-Trace" Orchestrator
ideas.description: This project involves building a web-based platform where users can define a sequence of 'cognitive skills' (e.g., information retrieval, reasoning, planning, action execution, verification) that an AI agent or a fleet of agents needs to perform for a given task. The orchestrator would then execute these tasks, capturing the trace of each agent's actions, the inputs it received, the outputs it produced, and the 'skill' it was attempting to utilize. The core value is visualizing the agent's workflow, identifying where it deviates, fails, or excels, enabling developers to debug and optimize agentic pipelines more effectively. It would include a simple dashboard to review traces and provide feedback/re-evaluation prompts.
tech_stack: LLM API (e.g., OpenAI, Claude, or local Llama.cpp via API)
ideas.source_inspiration: Cognitive-Core-Skills, a-fable-of-codexes, From Noisy Traces to Root Causes: Structural Trajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction for Agent Optimization, Show HN: Microsoft releases Flint, a visualization language for AI agents.
steps: Task 1: Initialize a Python FastAPI/Flask project for the backend.
Task 2: Design and implement a database schema for storing agent traces (task_id, step_order, skill_name, input_data, output_data, success_status, timestamp, error_message).
Task 3: Implement an interface to an LLM API (e.g., OpenAI or a local `llama.cpp` instance) to perform defined 'skills' based on prompts.
Task 4: Develop the core orchestration logic that takes a sequence of skill definitions, executes them with an LLM, and records detailed trace data.
Task 5: Build a simple web UI (React/Vue) to allow users to define agent tasks (e.g., via a JSON config or form for skill sequences) and trigger execution.
Task 6: Implement a dashboard component to display recorded traces, showing each step, its inputs, outputs, status, and potentially a flow visualization.

ideas.title: OmniPublish AI: Smart Content Orchestrator
ideas.description: This project involves creating a web application that acts as a central hub for generating, scheduling, and publishing content across multiple digital platforms (e.g., X, LinkedIn, a blog platform). Users define a topic or goal, and the system leverages an LLM to generate draft content tailored for different platforms (considering character limits, tone, hashtags, etc.). The 'smart' aspect comes from integrating a basic trend analysis module (e.g., by scraping simple news headlines or using a public trend API) to suggest popular topics or optimize post timing. It includes a scheduling calendar and analytics dashboard for published posts.
tech_stack: Social Media APIs (e.g., X API, LinkedIn API)
Web Scraper or Public News API (for trend data)
ideas.source_inspiration: creatorhub, OmniPost-AI, AMRouter (as a gateway concept for various APIs).
steps: Task 1: Set up a Python backend with FastAPI or Django REST Framework.
Task 2: Implement API calls to chosen LLM(s) for generating content drafts based on user prompts and desired platform characteristics.
Task 3: Develop integration modules for authenticating and publishing content to at least two target platforms (e.g., X, LinkedIn).
Task 4: Create a basic trend analysis service that fetches popular topics (e.g., via simple RSS feed parsing or a free public news API).
Task 5: Implement data models for storing generated content drafts, platform-specific variations, and scheduling information.
Task 6: Build a comprehensive frontend UI for prompting the LLM, reviewing/editing drafts, selecting platforms, scheduling posts, and viewing a content calendar.
Task 7: Implement a scheduled task runner (e.g., Celery, APScheduler) to automatically publish content at designated times.

ideas.title: Agentic Data Access Cache & Pre-fetcher
ideas.description: This project would build a middleware or proxy service that sits between analytical applications and a database. Instead of directly querying the database, applications would send their data requests to this proxy. An 'agent' within the proxy would analyze common query patterns and access behaviors. Using this analysis, it would intelligently pre-fetch and cache relevant datasets, potentially in an optimized format (e.g., columnar store like Parquet in memory or on disk), to serve subsequent, similar queries much faster. The agent could also use an SLM/LLM to interpret high-level data needs, predict future queries, or summarize frequently requested data points, thereby reducing the load on the primary database and 'breaking database lock-in' by providing a performance-optimized local view.
tech_stack: Local SLM (e.g., via `llama.cpp` server) for query interpretation (optional advanced feature)
ideas.source_inspiration: Breaking Database Lock-in: Agentic Regeneration of High Performance Storage Readers for Database Bypass, fucina (CPU-first execution implying local performance optimization).
steps: Task 1: Develop a FastAPI/Flask server to act as the data access proxy, exposing a simple API for data requests (e.g., `/query`, `/data`).
Task 2: Implement a robust database connector module to interface with a target SQL database (e.g., PostgreSQL).
Task 3: Create a query analyzer component that logs and analyzes incoming query patterns (tables, columns, filters, frequency).
Task 4: Integrate Redis or implement an efficient in-memory cache to store pre-fetched data, potentially serializing it into an optimized format like Parquet.
Task 5: Develop the 'agent' component that periodically analyzes query logs and proactively fetches data segments predicted to be needed soon, storing them in the cache.
Task 6: Implement query rewriting/routing logic: requests first check the cache; if data is available and fresh, serve from cache; otherwise, forward to DB, cache result, and serve.
Task 7: Add basic metrics logging to compare query response times with and without the proxy for performance benchmarking.

quick_actions: Action 1: Dedicate 30 minutes daily for the next week to thoroughly read the official documentation or a foundational paper (e.g., from arXiv) on a core AI/ML concept or framework you're interested in, focusing on deep understanding over superficial tutorials.

Action 2: Identify one personal or work-related digital task you perform manually more than once a week (e.g., file organization, data extraction from emails, routine social media posts) and build a small script or use an existing AI agent tool to automate a significant part of it.

Action 3: Experiment with setting up and running an open-source AI agent framework (e.g., LangChain, AutoGen) or a local SLM (e.g., using `llama.cpp` server) on your machine this week to understand its architecture and capabilities firsthand.

Action 4: Block out 30-60 minutes each week for a 'Reflection & Planning' session to review your completed tasks, identify workflow bottlenecks, and plan focused learning or automation efforts for the upcoming week, applying lessons from personal productivity trends.
